---
title: "Rust for bioinformaticians"
---

An intensive course teaching Rust to 4th-year biology students who have already taken one introductory programming course (R, Python, or — at worst — MATLAB). The unit of design is the **concept ↔ biology pairing**: every Rust feature is introduced together with a biological problem that motivates it.

## Where to start

- [**Audience**](audience.md) — who this course is for, and what we can and cannot assume they already know.
- [**Expected learning outcomes**](elo.md) — what students will be able to do at the end of each day.
- [**Design principles**](designprinciples.md) — how exercises are structured, and what we deliberately do not do.

## Before you start

- [**Install Rust, VS Code, and the plugins you need**](intro/install.qmd) — about 15 minutes on a fresh machine. Do this once, before day 1.

## Exercises by day

### Day 1 — Toolchain, scalars, control flow, functions

0. [Concepts — reference companion to the slides](day1/00-concepts.qmd)
1. [GC content](day1/01-gc-content.qmd)
2. [Complement a single base](day1/02-complement.qmd)
3. [Count A, C, G, T, and other](day1/03-base-counts.qmd)
4. [Phred quality scores](day1/04-phred.qmd)
5. [Hamming distance](day1/05-hamming.qmd)

### Day 2 — Ownership, borrowing, strings, slices, bytes

0. [Concepts — reference companion to the slides](day2/00-concepts.qmd)
1. [Reverse complement](day2/01-reverse-complement.qmd)
2. [Reverse complement into a buffer](day2/02-revcomp-buf.qmd)
3. [k-mers as slice views](day2/03-kmers.qmd)
4. [Quality trimming as a slice view](day2/04-quality-trim.qmd)

### Day 3 — Structs, enums, iterators, recursion, error handling

0. [Concepts — reference companion to the slides](day3/00-concepts.qmd)
1. [Genomic interval](day3/01-genomic-interval.qmd)
2. [Parsing a region string with `Result`](day3/02-parse-region.qmd)
3. [A codon table with `Option`](day3/03-codon-table.qmd)
4. [Counting k-mers with a `HashMap`](day3/04-kmer-counts.qmd)
5. [A phylogenetic tree with recursion](day3/05-phylo-tree.qmd)
6. [Sequence alignment with dynamic programming](day3/06-alignment.qmd)

### Day 4 — I/O, crates, modules, plotting, zip

0. [Concepts — reference companion to the slides](day4/00-concepts.qmd)
1. [Parsing BED by hand](day4/01-bed-parse.qmd)
2. [Per-contig stats with `noodles-fasta`](day4/02-fasta-stats.qmd)
3. [Filter FASTQ with `noodles-fastq`](day4/03-fastq-filter.qmd)
4. [Read-length histogram with `plotters`](day4/04-plot-readlen.qmd)
5. [Bundling outputs into a `.zip`](day4/05-zip-bundle.qmd)

### Day 5 — Tests, `--release`, parallelism, ecosystem

0. [Concepts — reference companion to the slides](day5/00-concepts.qmd)
1. [Use tests to find the bug](day5/01-find-the-bug.qmd)
2. [`--release` is a 50× free lunch](day5/02-release-mode.qmd)
3. [Parallel GC content with `rayon`](day5/03-parallel-gc.qmd)

### Day 6 — Yew & WebAssembly

A pure-Rust web app: Yew frontend (compiled to WebAssembly), axum backend, shared types crate — all in one cargo workspace.

0. [Concepts — reference companion to the slides](day6/00-concepts.qmd)
1. [Hello Yew](day6/01-hello-yew.qmd)
2. [Reactive sequence statistics](day6/02-reactive-stats.qmd)
3. [Fetch from the backend](day6/03-fetch-from-backend.qmd)
4. [Capstone — GC sliding window](day6/04-sequence-inspector.qmd)

### Day 7 — SQL & SQLite

Modelling data in relational form, querying it from the `sqlite` CLI, then reading and writing the same database from Rust.

0. [Concepts — reference companion to the slides](day7/00-concepts.qmd)
1. [Your first SQLite database](day7/01-sqlite-cli.qmd)
2. [A second table and a join](day7/02-natural-join.qmd)
3. [Reading a SQLite database from Rust](day7/03-read-from-rust.qmd)
4. [Writing a SQLite database from Rust](day7/04-write-from-rust.qmd)
5. [Joining two tables from Rust](day7/05-query-from-rust.qmd)

### Day 8 — Traits

The Rust mechanism for *shared interfaces* — `impl Trait for Type`, generic functions with trait bounds, and `Box<dyn Trait>` for heterogeneous collections.

0. [Concepts — reference companion to the slides](day8/00-concepts.qmd)
1. [Implement `Display` for `GenomicInterval`](day8/01-display.qmd)
2. [A custom trait with multiple impls](day8/02-annotate.qmd)
3. [A generic function with a trait bound](day8/03-generic-function.qmd)
4. [A `Box<dyn>` heterogeneous collection](day8/04-trait-objects.qmd)

### Day 9 — Algorithms and complexity

A short day on data-structure complexity (Big-O) and how to recognise it in code — two diagnostic quizzes that drive the lecture.

0. [Concepts — reference companion to the slides](day9/00-concepts.qmd)
1. [Warm-up quiz — data-structure complexity](day9/00a-ds-complexity-quiz.qmd)
2. [Warm-up quiz — complexity from code](day9/00b-code-complexity-quiz.qmd)

### Day 10 — Working with matrices

Dense and sparse matrix representations (`ndarray`, `sprs`), iterative linear-system solvers, and Markov chains via power iteration.

0. [Concepts — reference companion to the slides](day10/00-concepts.qmd)
1. [Loading and operating on a dense matrix](day10/01-dense-matrix.qmd)
2. [Sparse representations — memory comparison](day10/02-sparse-matrix.qmd)
3. [Implement Gauss-Seidel](day10/03-gauss-seidel.qmd)
4. [Markov chain — power iteration vs steady state](day10/04-markov-chain.qmd)

### Day 11 — GPUs and tensors with Burn

GPU-aware tensors via the [Burn](https://burn.dev/) framework, with every Rust example paired against the equivalent PyTorch / NumPy. Builds up to PCA via `nalgebra`, a PWM motif scanner as `Conv1d`, and a small trainable promoter classifier.

0. [Concepts — reference companion to the slides](day11/00-concepts.qmd)
1. [Tensors on any backend](day11/01-tensors.qmd)
2. [One-hot encoding DNA](day11/02-one-hot.qmd)
3. [GC content across a batch](day11/03-gc-content.qmd)
4. [Pairwise distance matrix](day11/04-distance-matrix.qmd)
5. [PCA with `nalgebra`](day11/05-pca.qmd)
6. [1-D convolutional motif scanner](day11/06-motif-scanner.qmd)
7. [Train a promoter classifier](day11/07-promoter-classifier.qmd)

## Working with the exercises

Each exercise pairs a short `.qmd` walkthrough with one or two real cargo projects:

- The walkthrough holds the prose, the hints (as collapsible callouts), and a reference solution snippet.
- The sibling `ex-<name>/` cargo project contains code that the Rust compiler will actually check, plus a `cargo test` spec that pins down the expected behaviour.
- The `ex-<name>-solution/` cargo project contains the reference solution as runnable code.

See the [design principles](designprinciples.md) for the full convention.

## Building this site

```bash
make           # render the whole site to _site/
make preview   # start a live-reload preview server
make clean     # delete _site/ and run `cargo clean` in every exercise crate
```

You will need [Quarto](https://quarto.org/docs/get-started/) and a recent [Rust toolchain](https://rustup.rs/) installed.
